title: Bounded Prefetch, Worker Ownership, Ordering, and Backpressure · Contract Triage
summary: A preserved Go Pokedex run contains one plausible command, parsing, URL, JSON, client, cache, ownership, goroutine, cancellation, persistence, security, test, packaging, or release defect; locate the first failed invariant before editing. Planned artifact: Repair a changed failure in bounded cancellable Pokemon prefetch pipeline and publish the reproduction, evidence, repair, and regression record. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, a…
objectives: Define which next-view latency benefit justifies prefetch traffic and when no prefetch should occur.
Set worker, queue, request, byte, and time ceilings from fair-use and resource evidence rather than input size.
Assign which goroutine creates, sends, closes, drains, cancels, and waits for each channel and result.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

content.text: A debug Go team handles case 2029 during a race-instrumented cache stress case. Build deterministic Go evidence to enforce prefetch concurrency, queue, ordering, cancellation, and partial-failure ownership; the fixed PokéAPI origin and fair-use traffic budget must remain enforced; then inject one redirect, control sequence, file, timeout, or persistence failure and reject it safely. Browser Go uses pure bounded models and original fixtures only. Real PokéAPI traffic, DNS, sockets, TLS, host files, terminals, signals, race and leak claims, load, cross-platform packaging, accessibility review, and production behavior require explicit controlled transfer gates. Competency probe: Use blocked workers, tiny queues, cancellation, duplicate jobs, partial failures, and race or leak evidence under deterministic fixtures.

steps.instruction:
Append a compile-ready pure-Go function headed "// Evidence: pokedex-go-pipeline-stress-tests-11hqmz" that uses case 1795 to enforce prefetch concurrency, queue, ordering, cancellation, and partial-failure ownership. Keep defaults runnable and return observable changed-case evidence. Browser code must not open sockets, resolve DNS, contact PokéAPI, read or write host files, handle real terminal or OS signals, spawn unbounded work, or claim race, leak, load, accessibility, cross-platform, package, or production behavior; verify those boundaries later with Go 1.26.5, original fixtures, controlled RoundTrippers and httptest servers, race and fuzz runs, temporary files, subprocess and signal harnesses, inspected artifacts, representative platforms, accessibility review, capacity, rollback, recovery, and release gates.

At Pokedex product engineering and Go Implement the cause-level repair · Debug Pokedex Go Pipeline Str…, trace this concrete input and predict values, errors, blocking, and output before reviewing it: A debug Go team handles case 5360 during a narrow-terminal ranger workflow. Build deterministic Go evidence to enforce prefetch concurrency, queue, ordering, cancellation, and partial-failure ownership; failed and canceled commands must preserve the prior admitted state; then change one resource identity, pagination link, JSON field, status, or body budget and revalidate admission. Browser Go uses pure bounded models and original fixtures only. Real PokéAPI traffic, DNS, sockets, TLS, host files, terminals, signals, race and leak claims, load, cross-platform packaging, accessibility review, and production behavior require explicit controlled transfer gates. Competency probe: Use blocked workers, tiny queues, cancellation, duplicate jobs, partial failures, and race or leak evidence under deterministic fixtures.

For Pokedex product engineering and Go Implement the cause-level repair · Debug Pokedex Go Pipeline Str…, challenge the documented misconception with runtime, failure, race, or toolchain evidence: A fast all-success pipeline test exercises backpressure.
